problem_text: BluePeak Batteries is piloting bike rentals in Providence. Weekly demand is Qd = 142 - 3P and supply is Qs = 21 + 2P, where P is the price per unit. After a campus partnership is announced, market research predicts demand will be 19 units higher at every price. Treat the market as competitive and use this setting to separate the arithmetic of equilibrium from the economic intuition.
solution:
(a) Set 142 - 3P = 21 + 2P. The initial price is $24.20 and quantity is 69.40.

(b) New demand is Qd = 161 - 3P, so P = $28 and Q = 77.

(c) Both sides trade more: quantity rises from 69.40 to 77.

(d) Demand rose at every price, creating excess demand at the old price. Competition bids price up and expands quantity.

problem_text: Cambridge is considering a seller tax on fitness classes. Before the tax, demand is Qd = 171 - 3P and supply is Qs = 25 + 2P. The proposed policy charges sellers $10 for each unit sold. Local businesses argue they will pay the entire tax, while student groups argue that buyers will face higher prices. Use the model to evaluate both claims.
solution:
(a) Solving 171 - 3P = 25 + 2P gives P = $29.20 and Q = 83.40.

(b) With Ps = Pc - 10, set 171 - 3Pc = 25 + 2(Pc - 10). Pc = $33.20, Ps = $23.20, and Q = 71.40.

(c) Revenue is tax times quantity: $10 x 71.40 = $714.

(d) Buyers pay $4 more per unit and sellers receive $6 less per unit.

(e) The seller writes the check, but market prices adjust. Incidence depends on supply and demand responses, not on who remits the tax.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: Revenue is tax times quantity: $10 x 71.40 = $714.

solution_subparts.label: d

solution_subparts.label: e
solution_subparts.text: The seller writes the check, but market prices adjust. Incidence depends on supply and demand responses, not on who remits the tax.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: Compute the pre-tax equilibrium price and quantity.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Compute the buyer price, seller price, and quantity after the tax.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Compute tax revenue.

subparts.label: d
subparts.text: How much of the tax is borne by buyers and how much by sellers?

subparts.label: e
subparts.text: Use your answer to explain why legal liability and economic incidence are not the same thing.

problem_text: Albany is considering a seller tax on software seats. Before the tax, demand is Qd = 172 - 3P and supply is Qs = 26 + 2P. The proposed policy charges sellers $11 for each unit sold. Local businesses argue they will pay the entire tax, while student groups argue that buyers will face higher prices. Use the model to evaluate both claims.
solution:
(a) Solving 172 - 3P = 26 + 2P gives P = $29.20 and Q = 84.40.

(b) With Ps = Pc - 11, set 172 - 3Pc = 26 + 2(Pc - 11). Pc = $33.60, Ps = $22.60, and Q = 71.20.

(c) Revenue is tax times quantity: $11 x 71.20 = $783.20.

(d) Buyers pay $4.40 more per unit and sellers receive $6.60 less per unit.

(e) The seller writes the check, but market prices adjust. Incidence depends on supply and demand responses, not on who remits the tax.

problem_text: Burlington is considering a seller tax on specialty noodles. Before the tax, demand is Qd = 173 - 3P and supply is Qs = 18 + 2P. The proposed policy charges sellers $6 for each unit sold. Local businesses argue they will pay the entire tax, while student groups argue that buyers will face higher prices. Use the model to evaluate both claims.
solution:
(a) Solving 173 - 3P = 18 + 2P gives P = $31 and Q = 80.

(b) With Ps = Pc - 6, set 173 - 3Pc = 18 + 2(Pc - 6). Pc = $33.40, Ps = $27.40, and Q = 72.80.

(c) Revenue is tax times quantity: $6 x 72.80 = $436.80.

(d) Buyers pay $2.40 more per unit and sellers receive $3.60 less per unit.

(e) The seller writes the check, but market prices adjust. Incidence depends on supply and demand responses, not on who remits the tax.

problem_text: Worcester is considering a seller tax on coffee subscriptions. Before the tax, demand is Qd = 188 - 3P and supply is Qs = 24 + 2P. The proposed policy charges sellers $9 for each unit sold. Local businesses argue they will pay the entire tax, while student groups argue that buyers will face higher prices. Use the model to evaluate both claims.
solution:
(a) Solving 188 - 3P = 24 + 2P gives P = $32.80 and Q = 89.60.

(b) With Ps = Pc - 9, set 188 - 3Pc = 24 + 2(Pc - 9). Pc = $36.40, Ps = $27.40, and Q = 78.80.

(c) Revenue is tax times quantity: $9 x 78.80 = $709.20.

(d) Buyers pay $3.60 more per unit and sellers receive $5.40 less per unit.

(e) The seller writes the check, but market prices adjust. Incidence depends on supply and demand responses, not on who remits the tax.

problem_text: A city council is studying the market for bike rentals after two simultaneous changes. Demand in Providence rises by 23 units at every price because commuting patterns change. At the same time, a supplier shortage lowers supply by 13 units at every price. Initially Qd = 221 - 4P and Qs = 21 + 2P. After the shock, the council considers a buyer subsidy of $6 per unit to keep the market active.
solution:
(a) Initial equilibrium is P = $33.33 and Q = 87.67.

(b) Post-shock demand is Qd = 244 - 4P and supply is Qs = 8 + 2P, so P = $39.33 and Q = 86.67.

(c) The subsidy implies Ps = Pb + 6. Solving gives Pb = $37.33, Ps = $43.33, and Q = 94.67.

(d) Government spending is $6 x 94.67 = $568.

(e) Buyers pay less out of pocket and sellers receive more per unit; the benefit is shared through price adjustments.

(f) The subsidy creates a wedge that makes purchases look cheaper to buyers and sales more attractive to sellers, so additional trades occur.

problem_text: A city council is studying the market for fitness classes after two simultaneous changes. Demand in Cambridge rises by 23 units at every price because commuting patterns change. At the same time, a supplier shortage lowers supply by 14 units at every price. Initially Qd = 226 - 4P and Qs = 19 + 2P. After the shock, the council considers a buyer subsidy of $6 per unit to keep the market active.
solution:
(a) Initial equilibrium is P = $34.50 and Q = 88.

(b) Post-shock demand is Qd = 249 - 4P and supply is Qs = 5 + 2P, so P = $40.67 and Q = 86.33.

(c) The subsidy implies Ps = Pb + 6. Solving gives Pb = $38.67, Ps = $44.67, and Q = 94.33.

(d) Government spending is $6 x 94.33 = $566.

(e) Buyers pay less out of pocket and sellers receive more per unit; the benefit is shared through price adjustments.

(f) The subsidy creates a wedge that makes purchases look cheaper to buyers and sales more attractive to sellers, so additional trades occur.

problem_text: Crimson Kite experiments with the price of its coffee subscriptions. When price rises from $19 to $23, weekly sales fall from 523 to 472 units. Management wants more than a calculation: it wants to know whether this kind of price increase is a sensible revenue strategy.
solution:
(a) Elasticity is (472 - 523)/523 divided by (23 - 19)/19, which equals -0.46.

(b) The absolute value is 0.46, so demand is inelastic.

(c) Revenue changes from $9,937 to $10,856.

(d) Revenue increases, so the price increase succeeds on a revenue-only metric.

(b) The absolute value is 0.64, so demand is inelastic.

(c) Revenue changes from $12,788 to $13,338.

(d) Revenue increases, so the price increase succeeds on a revenue-only metric.

problem_text: Zenith Zips estimates demand for a monthly plan as Q = 272 - 4P. The current price is $28 and marginal cost is $18. A manager notices that students in class used both elasticity and markup rules to think about market power, and asks whether the current price is internally consistent with that logic.
solution:
(a) Q = 272 - 4(28) = 160.

(b) Point elasticity is (dQ/dP)(P/Q) = -4 x 28/160 = -0.70.

(c) The Lerner markup is (28 - 18)/28 = 0.36.

(d) The inverse-elasticity target is 1/0.70 = 1.43. The current markup is below that benchmark.

(e) In the inelastic region, a price increase raises revenue while reducing units and costs, so the firm would usually want to raise price.

problem_text: BluePeak Batteries estimates demand for a monthly plan as Q = 298 - 4P. The current price is $33 and marginal cost is $19. A manager notices that students in class used both elasticity and markup rules to think about market power, and asks whether the current price is internally consistent with that logic.
solution:
(a) Q = 298 - 4(33) = 166.

(b) Point elasticity is (dQ/dP)(P/Q) = -4 x 33/166 = -0.80.

(c) The Lerner markup is (33 - 19)/33 = 0.42.

(d) The inverse-elasticity target is 1/0.80 = 1.26. The current markup is below that benchmark.

(e) In the inelastic region, a price increase raises revenue while reducing units and costs, so the firm would usually want to raise price.

problem_text: Quantum Kettle is evaluating two pieces of evidence before changing the price of coffee subscriptions. In its own price test, price fell from $35 to $32 and quantity rose from 544 to 553. In the same month, a rival raised price from $19 to $22, and Quantum Kettle's sales in a comparable market rose from 682 to 771. The CEO wants a recommendation that separates own-price sensitivity from substitution against rivals.
solution:
(a) Arc elasticity is the percent quantity change over the percent price change using midpoints: -0.18.

(b) Revenue changes from $19,040 to $17,696.

(c) Cross-price elasticity is (771 - 682)/682 divided by (22 - 19)/19 = 0.83.

(d) The cross-price elasticity is positive, so the products are substitutes.

(e) Own-price elasticity measures movement along the firm's demand curve; cross-price elasticity measures how rival prices shift demand.

(f) If revenue rose after the price cut, lowering price may be attractive; the positive cross elasticity also means rival pricing should be monitored closely.

problem_text: Vertex Coffee is evaluating two pieces of evidence before changing the price of fitness classes. In its own price test, price fell from $32 to $29 and quantity rose from 556 to 562. In the same month, a rival raised price from $22 to $25, and Vertex Coffee's sales in a comparable market rose from 688 to 780. The CEO wants a recommendation that separates own-price sensitivity from substitution against rivals.
solution:
(a) Arc elasticity is the percent quantity change over the percent price change using midpoints: -0.11.

(b) Revenue changes from $17,792 to $16,298.

(c) Cross-price elasticity is (780 - 688)/688 divided by (25 - 22)/22 = 0.98.

(d) The cross-price elasticity is positive, so the products are substitutes.

(e) Own-price elasticity measures movement along the firm's demand curve; cross-price elasticity measures how rival prices shift demand.

(f) If revenue rose after the price cut, lowering price may be attractive; the positive cross elasticity also means rival pricing should be monitored closely.

problem_text: Hartford caps the price of portable batteries at $20 after complaints about affordability. Demand is Qd = 136 - 3P and supply is Qs = 16 + 2P. The policy is popular with some buyers, but the mayor wants to know whether it actually helps buyers get the product.
solution:
(a) Unregulated P = $24 and Q = 64.

(b) The ceiling is binding because $20 is below $24.

(c) At the ceiling, Qd = 76 and Qs = 56, so the shortage is 20.

(d) Some buyers pay less, but others cannot find units at all because quantity supplied falls below quantity demanded.

problem_text: Providence caps the price of campus shuttles at $20.60 after complaints about affordability. Demand is Qd = 145 - 3P and supply is Qs = 17 + 2P. The policy is popular with some buyers, but the mayor wants to know whether it actually helps buyers get the product.
solution:
(a) Unregulated P = $25.60 and Q = 68.20.

(b) The ceiling is binding because $20.60 is below $25.60.

(c) At the ceiling, Qd = 83.20 and Qs = 58.20, so the shortage is 25.

(d) Some buyers pay less, but others cannot find units at all because quantity supplied falls below quantity demanded.

problem_text: The state proposes a per-unit excise tax of $8 on meal kits sold by firms such as Brass Lantern. Market demand is Qd = 151 - 3P and supply is Qs = 15 + 2P. Opponents argue the tax is too high for firms to absorb and will create a large efficiency loss. Use the model to evaluate those two claims.
solution:
(a) Before the tax, P = $27.20 and Q = 69.40.

(b) After the tax, Pc = $30.40, Ps = $22.40, and Q = 59.80.

(c) Revenue is $478.40. DWL is 0.5 x $8 x 9.60 = $38.40.

(d) Buyers pay $3.20 more and sellers receive $4.80 less, so the larger change indicates the larger burden.

(e) If demand is inelastic, quantity falls by less, so fewer mutually beneficial trades are lost.

(d) Buyers pay $3.60 more and sellers receive $5.40 less, so the larger change indicates the larger burden.

(e) If demand is inelastic, quantity falls by less, so fewer mutually beneficial trades are lost.

problem_text: To stabilize incomes for local suppliers of portable batteries, Hartford considers a price floor of $37. Demand is Qd = 176 - 3P and supply is Qs = 16 + 2P. One proposal simply mandates the floor; another has the government buy any unsold units at the floor price. The council wants to understand the tradeoffs before voting.
solution:
(a) Competitive P = $32 and Q = 80.

(b) The floor is binding because $37 is above the competitive price.

(c) At the floor, Qd = 65 and Qs = 90, so excess supply is 25.

(d) The buyback cost is $37 x 25 = $925.

(e) Sellers who sell at the floor gain from a higher price; buyers lose from paying more and buying less.

(f) Extra units are produced even though buyers do not value them at the floor price, and a buyback can spend public funds on unsold output.

problem_text: To stabilize incomes for local suppliers of fitness classes, Worcester considers a price floor of $38. Demand is Qd = 177 - 3P and supply is Qs = 17 + 2P. One proposal simply mandates the floor; another has the government buy any unsold units at the floor price. The council wants to understand the tradeoffs before voting.
solution:
(a) Competitive P = $32 and Q = 81.

(b) The floor is binding because $38 is above the competitive price.

(c) At the floor, Qd = 63 and Qs = 93, so excess supply is 30.

(d) The buyback cost is $38 x 30 = $1,140.

(e) Sellers who sell at the floor gain from a higher price; buyers lose from paying more and buying less.

(f) Extra units are produced even though buyers do not value them at the floor price, and a buyback can spend public funds on unsold output.

problem_text: Worcester is small relative to the world market for fitness classes. Domestic demand is Qd = 91 - 2P and domestic supply is Qs = 11 + 1P. The world price is $20.67, and trade is initially free. The question is not only whether imports occur, but who is likely to favor free trade.
solution:
(a) Autarky P = $26.67 and Q = 37.67.

(b) At $20.67, Qd = 49.67 and Qs = 31.67.

(c) Since Qd > Qs, the country imports 18 units.

(d) Consumers gain from the lower price; domestic producers lose because they sell less at a lower price.

solution_subparts.text: Autarky P = $26.67 and Q = 37.67.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: Since Qd > Qs, the country imports 18 units.

solution_subparts.label: d
solution_subparts.text: Consumers gain from the lower price; domestic producers lose because they sell less at a lower price.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: Find the no-trade equilibrium price and quantity.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: At the world price, compute domestic consumption and domestic production.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Does the country import or export, and by how much?

subparts.label: d
subparts.text: Identify the domestic winners and losers from opening to trade.

problem_text: After a period of free trade in bike rentals, the government imposes an import tariff of $3 per unit. Domestic demand is Qd = 106 - 2P, domestic supply is Qs = 10 + 1P, and the world price is $26. Assume the country is small, so the world price itself does not change.
solution:
(a) Free-trade imports are 18 units.

(b) The domestic price is $29. Qd = 48, Qs = 39, so imports are 9.

(c) Tariff revenue is $3 x 9 = $27.

(d) Consumers lose from the higher price; domestic producers gain because they sell more at that higher price.

(e) Some lost consumer surplus becomes producer surplus or government revenue, but some becomes deadweight loss from inefficiently low consumption and high domestic production.

problem_text: Lawmakers are comparing a tariff and a quota in the market for fitness classes. Domestic demand is Qd = 131 - 2P, domestic supply is Qs = 11 + 1P, and the world price is $34. A tariff of $6 would raise the domestic price to $40. An alternative quota would cap imports at 1 units.
solution:
(a) Free-trade imports are 18.

problem_text: LoomLabs produces software seats with daily cost C(q) = 124 + 13q + q^2. A team member wants to price by dividing total cost by output, while another argues that the next unit should be evaluated using marginal cost. Use q = 9 to clarify the difference.
solution:
(a) C(9) = $322.

(b) ATC = $322/9 = $35.78. AVC = (13q + q^2)/q = $22.

(c) MC(q) = 13 + 2q, so MC(9) = $31.

(d) The one-more-unit decision uses marginal cost, because fixed and already-incurred costs do not change with that unit.

solution_subparts.label: d
solution_subparts.text: The one-more-unit decision uses marginal cost, because fixed and already-incurred costs do not change with that unit.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: Compute total cost at q.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Compute average total cost and average variable cost at q.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Compute marginal cost at q.

subparts.label: d
subparts.text: Explain which cost concept is relevant for deciding whether to produce one more unit.

problem_text: Quantum Kettle produces coffee subscriptions with daily cost C(q) = 124 + 14q + q^2. A team member wants to price by dividing total cost by output, while another argues that the next unit should be evaluated using marginal cost. Use q = 9 to clarify the difference.
solution:
(a) C(9) = $331.

(b) ATC = $331/9 = $36.78. AVC = (14q + q^2)/q = $23.

(c) MC(q) = 14 + 2q, so MC(9) = $32.

(d) The one-more-unit decision uses marginal cost, because fixed and already-incurred costs do not change with that unit.

problem_text: Elm City Foods is a small producer in a competitive market for standing desks. Its short-run cost is C(q) = 148 + 18q + q^2, and the market price is $46. The fixed cost is unavoidable this month, but not in the long run. Management asks whether the firm should produce now and whether the industry should expect entry or exit later.
solution:
(a) MC = 18 + 2q. Setting 46 = 18 + 2q gives q = 14.

(b) Profit is Pq - C(q) = $48.

(c) The firm produces because price exceeds minimum AVC, which is $18 for this cost function.

(d) ATC = 148/q + 18 + q, minimized at q = sqrt(148); min ATC = $42.33.

(e) Since price is above min ATC, long-run forces point toward entry and falling price.

problem_text: Vertex Coffee is a small producer in a competitive market for fitness classes. Its short-run cost is C(q) = 144 + 18q + q^2, and the market price is $46. The fixed cost is unavoidable this month, but not in the long run. Management asks whether the firm should produce now and whether the industry should expect entry or exit later.
solution:
(a) MC = 18 + 2q. Setting 46 = 18 + 2q gives q = 14.

(b) Profit is Pq - C(q) = $52.

(c) The firm produces because price exceeds minimum AVC, which is $18 for this cost function.

(d) ATC = 144/q + 18 + q, minimized at q = sqrt(144); min ATC = $42.

(e) Since price is above min ATC, long-run forces point toward entry and falling price.

problem_text: Vanta Vacuum can produce software seats using labor L and machine time K according to q = sqrt(LK). Labor costs $17 per unit and machine time costs $30 per unit. The operations team needs q = 21 units for a contract and asks whether simply using equal amounts of L and K is cost-minimizing.
solution:
(a) Minimize 17L + 30K subject to sqrt(LK) = 21, or LK = 441.

(b) The tangency condition gives K/L = w/r = 0.57.

(c) L = q sqrt(r/w) = 27.90 and K = q sqrt(w/r) = 15.81.

(d) Minimized cost is $474.25 + $474.25 = $948.49.

(e) The firm should substitute away from labor and toward machine time. Since K/L = w/r, a higher labor wage raises the machine-time-to-labor ratio.

(f) Cost minimization equalizes marginal product per dollar, not raw units. Input prices matter.

problem_text: Meridian Micro is one of many small sellers of specialty noodles. Its cost is C(q) = 104 + 21q + q^2, and the market price this week is $40. The owner understands that she is a price taker but is unsure how that translates into an output decision.
solution:
(a) A price-taking firm can sell another unit at the market price, so MR = P.

(b) Set P = MC: 40 = 21 + 2q, so q = 9.50.

(c) Profit is -$13.75.

(d) If entry is free and firms are similar, positive economic profit attracts entry and pushes price down.

problem_text: A competitive market for coffee subscriptions has become crowded. A representative firm has C(q) = 116 + 21q + q^2 and currently faces price $33. The fixed cost is sunk in the short run. Students often confuse producing at a loss with exiting, so answer separately for the short run and the long run.
solution:
(a) q = (33 - 21)/2 = 6.

(b) Profit is -$80.

(c) Yes, because P = $33 is above minimum AVC = $21, so production helps cover fixed cost.

(d) Minimum ATC is $42.54.

(e) Since price is below break-even, firms exit until price moves toward min ATC.

problem_text: A competitive market for bike rentals has become crowded. A representative firm has C(q) = 100 + 24q + q^2 and currently faces price $37. The fixed cost is sunk in the short run. solution:

(b) Profit is -$57.75.

(c) Yes, because P = $37 is above minimum AVC = $24, so production helps cover fixed cost.

(d) Minimum ATC is $44.

problem_text: There are 19 identical competitive firms producing specialty noodles. Each has C(q) = 120 + 21q + q^2, and market demand is Qd = 684 - 3P. Assume firms with positive output follow P = MC in the short run. The industry association wants to know how a short-run equilibrium differs from a long-run equilibrium with entry and exit.
solution:
(a) P = 21 + 2q, so q = (P - 21)/2 for P > $21.

(b) Industry supply is Qs = 19(P - 21)/2.

(c) Solving Qd = Qs gives P = $70.68 and Q = 471.96.

(d) Each firm produces 24.84 and earns profit $497.03.

(e) Minimum ATC is $42.91, so price is above break-even.

(f) Firms will enter, putting downward pressure on price.

problem_text: Nebula Noodles is the only provider of a specialized home sensors service in its region. Inverse demand is P = 121 - 2Q, marginal cost is constant at $19, and fixed cost is $85. The firm wants to understand why a monopolist does not simply serve every customer with willingness to pay above marginal cost.
solution:
(a) Revenue is 121Q - 2Q^2, so MR = 121 - 4Q.

(b) Set MR = MC: Q = 25.50 and P = $70.

(c) Profit is ($70 - $19) x 25.50 - $85 = $1,215.50.

(d) Selling more requires lowering price on marginal and inframarginal units, so the monopolist restricts quantity to keep price above MC.

problem_text: Zenith Zips is the only provider of a specialized specialty noodles service in its region. Inverse demand is P = 130 - 2Q, marginal cost is constant at $21, and fixed cost is $80. The firm wants to understand why a monopolist does not simply serve every customer with willingness to pay above marginal cost.
solution:
(a) Revenue is 130Q - 2Q^2, so MR = 130 - 4Q.

(b) Set MR = MC: Q = 27.25 and P = $75.50.

(c) Profit is ($75.50 - $21) x 27.25 - $80 = $1,405.12.

(d) Selling more requires lowering price on marginal and inframarginal units, so the monopolist restricts quantity to keep price above MC.

problem_text: Juniper Labs has local monopoly power over campus shuttles. Demand is P = 136 - 2Q and marginal cost is $20. A regulator asks students to compare the monopoly outcome with a competitive benchmark where price equals marginal cost.
solution:
(a) Monopoly Q = 29 and P = $78.

(b) Competitive price equals MC = $20 and Q = 58.

(c) Variable profit is $1,682; after fixed cost, profit is $1,597.

(d) DWL is 0.5 x ($78 - $20) x (58 - 29) = $841.

(e) Consumers with values between MC and the monopoly price are excluded; those lost trades would have created surplus.

problem_text: LoomLabs has local monopoly power over software seats. Demand is P = 149 - 2Q and marginal cost is $19. A regulator asks students to compare the monopoly outcome with a competitive benchmark where price equals marginal cost.
solution:
(a) Monopoly Q = 32.50 and P = $84.

(b) Competitive price equals MC = $19 and Q = 65.

(c) Variable profit is $2,112.50; after fixed cost, profit is $2,012.50.

(d) DWL is 0.5 x ($84 - $19) x (65 - 32.50) = $1,056.25.

(e) Consumers with values between MC and the monopoly price are excluded; those lost trades would have created surplus.

problem_text: A public debate over Atlas Droneworks' home sensors service considers two policies. Demand is P = 161 - 2Q, marginal cost is $24, and fixed cost is $85. Policy 1 is a per-unit tax of $7. Policy 2 is a price cap of $35. Assume the firm serves all demand at the cap if doing so covers marginal cost.
solution:
(a) Unregulated Q = 34.25 and P = $92.50.

(b) The tax raises effective MC to $31. Q = 32.50, P = $96, and after-tax profit is $2,027.50.

(c) At the cap, Qd = (161 - 35)/2 = 63.

(d) The cap quantity is higher than monopoly quantity (63 versus 34.25).

(e) The price cap is more likely to expand output because it lowers price toward marginal cost, while the tax raises effective marginal cost.

(f) A well-chosen cap can reduce deadweight loss, but a cap below cost could reduce service quality, create shortages, or discourage investment.

problem_text: Nova Basket sells a single-use pass to two customer groups. 121 casual customers are willing to pay $56, and 81 business customers are willing to pay $91. Marginal cost is $21. The firm cannot initially identify customers, so it must set one price.
solution:
(a) Profit is ($56 - $21) x 202 = $7,070.

(b) Profit is ($91 - $21) x 81 = $5,670.

(c) The firm chooses the low price because it gives higher profit.

(d) Different groups have different willingness to pay. Charging each group closer to its value would capture more surplus if arbitrage is prevented.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: The firm chooses the low price because it gives higher profit.

solution_subparts.label: d
solution_subparts.text: Different groups have different willingness to pay. Charging each group closer to its value would capture more surplus if arbitrage is prevented.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: Compute profit from setting the low price and serving both groups.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Compute profit from setting the high price and serving only business customers.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Which uniform price should the firm choose?

subparts.label: d
subparts.text: Explain why the firm would like to price discriminate if it could prevent resale.

(d) Different groups have different willingness to pay. Charging each group closer to its value would capture more surplus if arbitrage is prevented.

problem_text: Juniper Labs sells a single-use pass to two customer groups. 121 casual customers are willing to pay $58, and 80 business customers are willing to pay $97. Marginal cost is $23. The firm cannot initially identify customers, so it must set one price.
solution:
(a) Profit is ($58 - $23) x 201 = $7,035.

(b) Profit is ($97 - $23) x 80 = $5,920.

(c) The firm chooses the low price because it gives higher profit.

(d) Different groups have different willingness to pay. Charging each group closer to its value would capture more surplus if arbitrage is prevented.

problem_text: Keystone Transit can sell a basic and premium version of a service. There are 100 flexible users and 100 high-value users. Flexible users value basic at $45 and premium at $62. High-value users value basic at $57 and premium at $90. Costs are $16 for basic and $25 for premium. The firm cannot observe type.
solution:
(a) The basic price can be $45.

(b) High-value users need 90 - Ppremium >= 57 - 45, so Ppremium = $78.

(c) Menu profit is 100($45 - $16) + 100($78 - $25) = $8,200.

(d) Premium-only profit is 100($90 - $25) = $6,500.

(e) If the premium price extracts all high-type surplus, high types may prefer the basic version. The firm leaves rents to preserve self-selection.

problem_text: Brass Lantern can prevent resale between two markets. In market A, inverse demand is P_A = 151 - 2Q_A. In market B, inverse demand is P_B = 121 - 1Q_B. Marginal cost is $21 in both markets. The firm is considering third-degree price discrimination.
solution:
(a) MR_A = 151 - 4Q_A and MR_B = 121 - 2Q_B.

(b) Market A: Q = 32.50 and P = $86.

(c) Market B: Q = 50 and P = $71.

(d) Total variable profit is $4,612.50.

(e) The higher price is in market A, indicating relatively less elastic demand at the chosen quantity.

(f) The firm must be able to segment customers and prevent profitable resale/arbitrage.

(e) The higher price is in market A, indicating relatively less elastic demand at the chosen quantity.

(f) The firm must be able to segment customers and prevent profitable resale/arbitrage.

problem_text: A graduating student compares two jobs. Job Safe pays $93 thousand for sure. Job Bonus pays $71 thousand plus a $37 thousand bonus with probability 60.0%. Payoffs are measured in thousands of dollars, and the student has utility u(x) = sqrt(x).
solution:
(a) EV(Bonus) = 71 + 0.6 x 37 = $93.20 thousand.

(b) A risk-neutral student chooses Job Bonus because it has the higher expected value.

(c) EU(Bonus) = 9.61 and U(Safe) = 9.64.

(d) Risk-averse utility is concave, so downside risk lowers expected utility relative to a sure payment with the same expected value.

subparts.text: Compute the expected monetary value of Job Bonus.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Which job would a risk-neutral student choose?

subparts.label: d
subparts.text: Explain why risk aversion can change the ranking even when expected value is higher.

problem_text: A graduating student compares two jobs. Job Safe pays $98 thousand for sure. Job Bonus pays $72 thousand plus a $41 thousand bonus with probability 60.0%. Payoffs are measured in thousands of dollars, and the student has utility u(x) = sqrt(x).

(c) EU(Bonus) = 9.77 and U(Safe) = 9.90.

(d) Risk-averse utility is concave, so downside risk lowers expected utility relative to a sure payment with the same expected value.

problem_text: A small business has wealth of $250 thousand. A flood would cause a loss of $91 thousand with probability 2.0%. The owner has utility u(x) = ln(x), with x in thousands. Full insurance is available for a premium of $2.82 thousand.
solution:
(a) EV uninsured is $248.18 thousand.

(b) CE solves ln(CE) = 2.0% ln(159) + 98.0% ln(250), so CE = $247.75 thousand.

(c) Maximum premium for full insurance is $2.25 thousand.

(d) The owner does not buy because the offered premium is above willingness to pay.

(e) Insurance removes risk. A risk-averse person may accept a lower expected monetary value in exchange for a less variable outcome.

problem_text: A small business has wealth of $250 thousand. A flood would cause a loss of $90 thousand with probability 3.0%. The owner has utility u(x) = ln(x), with x in thousands. Full insurance is available for a premium of $3.70 thousand.
solution:
(a) EV uninsured is $247.30 thousand.

(b) CE solves ln(CE) = 3.0% ln(160) + 97.0% ln(250), so CE = $246.68 thousand.

(c) Maximum premium for full insurance is $3.32 thousand.

(d) The owner does not buy because the offered premium is above willingness to pay.

(e) Insurance removes risk. A risk-averse person may accept a lower expected monetary value in exchange for a less variable outcome.

problem_text: A firm offers analysts a risky compensation package: base salary $91 thousand and a bonus of $64 thousand with probability 75.0%. A competing firm offers a guaranteed $111 thousand. Analysts have utility u(x)=sqrt(x). The firm is considering a smoother package with the same expected value but a smaller bonus.
solution:
(a) EV = $139 thousand.

(b) CE = EU^2 = $137.41 thousand.

(c) The analyst prefers the risky package because CE is above $111 thousand.

(d) Using a $20 thousand expected bonus component gives bonus $26.67 thousand and base $119 thousand.

(e) EU original is 11.72; EU smoother is 11.78. The smoother package is preferred by risk-averse workers.

(f) For risk-averse employees, the firm may need to pay a risk premium for volatile compensation; smoother pay can be valuable even at the same expected value.

subparts.text: Which offer does a risk-averse analyst prefer: the risky package or the guaranteed competing offer?

problem_text: A firm offers analysts a risky compensation package: base salary $89 thousand and a bonus of $61 thousand with probability 75.0%. A competing firm offers a guaranteed $115 thousand. Analysts have utility u(x)=sqrt(x). The firm is considering a smoother package with the same expected value but a smaller bonus.
solution:
(a) EV = $134.75 thousand.

(b) CE = EU^2 = $133.27 thousand.

(c) The analyst prefers the risky package because CE is above $115 thousand.

(d) Using a $20 thousand expected bonus component gives bonus $26.67 thousand and base $114.75 thousand.

(e) EU original is 11.54; EU smoother is 11.60. The smoother package is preferred by risk-averse workers.

(f) For risk-averse employees, the firm may need to pay a risk premium for volatile compensation; smoother pay can be valuable even at the same expected value.

problem_text: Buyers in a used-standing desks market cannot tell high-quality units from low-quality units before purchase. High-quality units are worth $121 to buyers and cost sellers $86 to part with. Low-quality units are worth $61 and cost sellers $46. Buyers believe 60.0% of units are high quality.
problem_title: Quality Uncertainty in a Used Market
solution:
(a) Pooled WTP is 60.0% x $121 + 40.0% x $61 = $97.

(b) High-quality sellers sell; low-quality sellers sell because $97 exceeds $46.

(c) If only low-quality units remain, WTP falls to $61.

(d) The market pool worsens as price attracts low-quality sellers and discourages high-quality sellers, reducing gains from trade.

solution_subparts.label: b
solution_subparts.text: High-quality sellers sell; low-quality sellers sell because $97 exceeds $46.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: If only low-quality units remain, WTP falls to $61.

solution_subparts.label: d
solution_subparts.text: The market pool worsens as price attracts low-quality sellers and discourages high-quality sellers, reducing gains from trade.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: Compute buyers' willingness to pay for a randomly selected unit.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: At that pooled price, which sellers are willing to sell?

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: If high-quality sellers leave, what happens to buyers' willingness to pay?

subparts.label: d
subparts.text: Explain why this is an adverse selection problem.

(b) High-quality sellers sell; low-quality sellers sell because $97.80 exceeds $46.

(c) If only low-quality units remain, WTP falls to $63.

(d) The market pool worsens as price attracts low-quality sellers and discourages high-quality sellers, reducing gains from trade.

problem_text: Maple Circuit sells refurbished software seats. Without a warranty, units sell for $78. A high-quality seller expects no repair cost from a warranty, while a low-quality seller expects warranty claims of $29. A proposed certified listing includes the warranty and sells for $114.
solution:
(a) No-warranty payoffs are $7 for high quality and $26 for low quality.

(b) Warranty payoffs are $43 for high quality and $33 for low quality.

(c) The type with the higher relative payoff from warranty listing will offer it; with these numbers, high quality benefits more.

(d) The signal works because it is less costly for high-quality sellers to provide than for low-quality sellers.

(e) Claims abuse, weak enforcement, exclusions, or seller bankruptcy can make warranties less credible.

problem_text: An insurer cannot observe whether drivers are safe or risky. Safe drivers have accident probability 1.0%; risky drivers have probability 5.0%. An accident costs $100 thousand, and all drivers have wealth $250 thousand with utility u(x)=ln(x). The insurer considers a full-insurance plan with premium $5 thousand and a partial plan with premium $1 thousand and deductible $71 thousand.
solution:
(a) Expected costs are $1 thousand for safe drivers and $5 thousand for risky drivers.

(b) Safe EU full = 5.50; safe EU partial = 5.51.

(c) Risky EU full = 5.50; risky EU partial = 5.50.

(d) Safe drivers are more likely to choose the high-deductible plan because they are less likely to pay the deductible.

(e) The menu screens customers: low-risk types self-select into lower premiums with more exposure, while high-risk types value full coverage more.

(f) Risk remains, some efficient coverage may be distorted to preserve self-selection, and the insurer still cannot directly observe type.

solution_subparts.text: Risky EU full = 5.50; risky EU partial = 5.50.

problem_text: Quantum Kettle employs sales associates who can either help customers or hide in the stockroom. Helping creates a sale with probability 75.0%; hiding creates a sale with probability 25.0%. Effort costs the worker $4 per hour, and the outside wage is $13 per hour. Workers are risk-neutral.
solution:
(a) Helping payoff is w + S b - c.

(b) Hiding payoff is w + n b.

(c) Need (S - n)b >= c, so b = 4/(0.75 - 0.25) = $8.

(d) With a flat wage, the worker bears the effort cost but does not receive a higher payoff when effort raises the chance of sale.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: Helping payoff is w + S b - c.

solution_subparts.label: b
solution_subparts.text: Hiding payoff is w + n b.

solution_subparts.label: d
solution_subparts.text: With a flat wage, the worker bears the effort cost but does not receive a higher payoff when effort raises the chance of sale.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: Write the worker's expected payoff from helping under wage w and bonus b.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Write the worker's expected payoff from hiding.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Find the minimum bonus that makes helping incentive compatible.

subparts.label: d
subparts.text: Explain why a flat wage would not solve the hidden-action problem.

problem_text: Oak Street Games is designing a bonus contract. If an employee works hard, the success probability is 75.0%; if the employee shirks, it is 25.0%. Effort costs $3, the outside option is $13, and workers are risk-neutral. Each success creates margin $9 for the firm before labor compensation.
solution:
(a) b* = c/(S - n) = 3/(0.75 - 0.25) = $6.

(b) w* = m - S b* + c = $11.50.

(c) Expected worker payoff is w + S b - c = $13, exactly the outside option.

(d) Firm profit with incentives is -$9.25 per worker-period; flat-wage profit is -$10.75. Incentives are worthwhile here.

(e) Noisier measurement weakens the link between effort and reward; risk aversion makes bonus pay costly because workers require compensation for income risk.

problem_text: Harbor Analytics can use the same risk-neutral bonus contract in two departments. In both departments, effort raises success probability from 25.0% to 75.0% and costs the worker $4. The outside wage is $13. The margin from success is $7 in Department L and $13 in Department H.
problem_title: Should the Firm Use Bonus Pay?
solution:
(a) b* = $8.

(b) w* = $11.

(c) Effort is worth inducing when margin X >= c/(S - n) = $8.

(d) Department L margin is $7, so incentives are not worthwhile.

(e) Department H margin is $13, so incentives are worthwhile.

problem_text: Sapphire Solar and Summit Seltzer simultaneously choose a high price or a low price. If both choose High, each earns $2,410. If one chooses Low while the other chooses High, the low-price firm earns $2,710 and the high-price firm earns $1,205. If both choose Low, each earns $1,805.
solution:
(a) Low is better against High because $2,710 > $2,410.

(b) Low is better against Low because $1,805 > $1,205.

(c) Low is a dominant strategy for both firms, so the Nash equilibrium is Low/Low.

(d) Each firm has an individual incentive to cut price, even though both would earn more if both could commit to High.

problem_text: Elm City Foods and Harbor Analytics simultaneously choose a high price or a low price. If both choose High, each earns $2,490. If one chooses Low while the other chooses High, the low-price firm earns $2,790 and the high-price firm earns $1,245. If both choose Low, each earns $1,845.
solution:
(a) Low is better against High because $2,790 > $2,490.

(b) Low is better against Low because $1,845 > $1,245.

(c) Low is a dominant strategy for both firms, so the Nash equilibrium is Low/Low.

(d) Each firm has an individual incentive to cut price, even though both would earn more if both could commit to High.

problem_text: Sterling Health and Aurora Scooters operate local training centers. Each can charge $50 or $40. At current capacity, the payoff matrix in weekly profit is: both 50 -> (3000, 3000); Sterling Health 50 and Aurora Scooters 40 -> (1800, 3200); Sterling Health 40 and Aurora Scooters 50 -> (3200, 1800); both 40 -> (2400, 2400). Aurora Scooters can expand capacity at fixed cost $300, changing its payoff from charging 40 against 50 to $3,100 and from both charging 40 to $2,600.
solution:
(a) Before expansion, each firm prefers 40 regardless of the rival's price, so the equilibrium is 40/40 with profit 2400 each.

(b) Firm 2's low-price payoff after expansion is $3,100 against 50 and $2,600 against 40; firm 1 still prefers 40 in both cases.

(c) The equilibrium remains 40/40 unless expansion makes firm 2 prefer 50 when firm 1 charges 40; with these payoffs it still chooses 40.

(d) Firm 2 earns $2,600 after expansion versus $2,400 before, so expansion raises its equilibrium profit.

(e) More capacity makes it more tempting to cut price to fill seats, which can intensify competition and reduce margins.

(a) Before expansion, each firm prefers 40 regardless of the rival's price, so the equilibrium is 40/40 with profit 2400 each.

(b) Firm 2's low-price payoff after expansion is $3,000 against 50 and $2,500 against 40; firm 1 still prefers 40 in both cases.

(c) The equilibrium remains 40/40 unless expansion makes firm 2 prefer 50 when firm 1 charges 40; with these payoffs it still chooses 40.

(d) Firm 2 earns $2,500 after expansion versus $2,400 before, so expansion raises its equilibrium profit.

(e) More capacity makes it more tempting to cut price to fill seats, which can intensify competition and reduce margins.

problem_text: An entrant is deciding whether to enter Juniper Labs's market. If the entrant stays out, Juniper Labs earns $2,620 and the entrant earns 0. If the entrant enters, Juniper Labs can accommodate or fight. Accommodation gives Juniper Labs $1,510 and the entrant $1,310. Fighting gives Juniper Labs $810 and the entrant -$200. Consider both a simultaneous-entry story and a sequential game in which the entrant moves first and Juniper Labs responds after observing entry.
solution:
(a) Accommodation is better because $1,510 > $810.

(b) Knowing the incumbent will accommodate, the entrant enters because $1,310 > 0.

(c) Payoffs are incumbent $1,510 and entrant $1,310.

(d) No. A threat to fight is not credible if the incumbent would prefer to accommodate once entry occurs.

(e) It could build excess capacity, sign contracts, or make other commitments that change the payoff from fighting versus accommodating.

(f) Strategic plans must be sequentially rational: rivals look ahead to what a firm will actually want to do later.

problem_text: An entrant is deciding whether to enter Cobalt Cloud's market. If the entrant stays out, Cobalt Cloud earns $2,780 and the entrant earns 0. If the entrant enters, Cobalt Cloud can accommodate or fight. Accommodation gives Cobalt Cloud $1,590 and the entrant $1,390. Fighting gives Cobalt Cloud $890 and the entrant -$200. Consider both a simultaneous-entry story and a sequential game in which the entrant moves first and Cobalt Cloud responds after observing entry.
solution:
(a) Accommodation is better because $1,590 > $890.

(b) Knowing the incumbent will accommodate, the entrant enters because $1,390 > 0.

(c) Payoffs are incumbent $1,590 and entrant $1,390.

(d) No. A threat to fight is not credible if the incumbent would prefer to accommodate once entry occurs.

(e) It could build excess capacity, sign contracts, or make other commitments that change the payoff from fighting versus accommodating.

(f) Strategic plans must be sequentially rational: rivals look ahead to what a firm will actually want to do later.

problem_text: Vertex Coffee and Vanta Vacuum sell identical meal kits and choose prices simultaneously. Each has constant marginal cost $21 and enough capacity to serve the whole market. Suppose one firm is currently charging $34.
solution:
(a) A firm can charge just below $34 and capture the market while still pricing above cost.

(b) Each firm wants to undercut a rival price above MC, so mutual high prices are not best responses.

(c) The Bertrand equilibrium price is $21, equal to marginal cost.

(d) Differentiation or capacity limits soften competition because a firm cannot necessarily capture all customers by undercutting.

problem_text: BluePeak Batteries and Cobalt Cloud choose quantities of portable batteries simultaneously. Market inverse demand is P = 136 - Q, where Q is total output. Both firms have constant marginal cost $24. They are considering whether competition or collusion is more profitable.
solution:
(a) Firm 1 maximizes (a - q1 - q2 - c)q1, so q1 = (112 - q2)/2.

(b) Symmetry gives q = (a - c)/3 = 37.33 for each firm.

(c) Price is $61.33 and profit per firm is $1,393.78.

(d) Monopoly total quantity is 56 and price is $80.

(e) Each firm may gain by secretly expanding output while the other restricts output, so collusion requires enforcement.

problem_text: Cobalt Cloud and Crimson Kite choose quantities of fitness classes simultaneously. Market inverse demand is P = 137 - Q, where Q is total output. Both firms have constant marginal cost $25. They are considering whether competition or collusion is more profitable.
solution:
(a) Firm 1 maximizes (a - q1 - q2 - c)q1, so q1 = (112 - q2)/2.

(b) Symmetry gives q = (a - c)/3 = 37.33 for each firm.

(c) Price is $62.33 and profit per firm is $1,393.78.

(d) Monopoly total quantity is 56 and price is $81.

(e) Each firm may gain by secretly expanding output while the other restricts output, so collusion requires enforcement.

problem_text: Zenith Zips and Atlas Droneworks choose quantities of standing desks simultaneously. Market inverse demand is P = 153 - Q, where Q is total output. Both firms have constant marginal cost $23. They are considering whether competition or collusion is more profitable.
solution:
(a) Firm 1 maximizes (a - q1 - q2 - c)q1, so q1 = (130 - q2)/2.

(b) Symmetry gives q = (a - c)/3 = 43.33 for each firm.

(c) Price is $66.33 and profit per firm is $1,877.78.

(d) Monopoly total quantity is 65 and price is $88.

(e) Each firm may gain by secretly expanding output while the other restricts output, so collusion requires enforcement.

problem_text: Long Wharf Roasters can commit to capacity before Maple Circuit chooses output in the market for meal kits. Demand is P = 161 - Q and both firms have marginal cost $25. Compare this Stackelberg situation with simultaneous Cournot competition.
solution:
(a) Follower best response is q2 = (136 - q1)/2.

(b) Leader quantity is 68 and follower quantity is 34.

(c) Price is $59; leader profit is $2,312 and follower profit is $1,156.

(d) Cournot has each firm produce 45.33 and price $70.33.

(e) The leader benefits by committing to a larger quantity, forcing the follower to scale back.

(f) A credible early capacity choice changes the rival's best response and can shift profit toward the committed firm.

problem_text: Crimson Kite can commit to capacity before Echo Forge chooses output in the market for software seats. Demand is P = 168 - Q and both firms have marginal cost $20. Compare this Stackelberg situation with simultaneous Cournot competition.
solution:
(a) Follower best response is q2 = (148 - q1)/2.

(b) Leader quantity is 74 and follower quantity is 37.

(c) Price is $57; leader profit is $2,738 and follower profit is $1,369.

(d) Cournot has each firm produce 49.33 and price $69.33.

(e) The leader benefits by committing to a larger quantity, forcing the follower to scale back.

(f) A credible early capacity choice changes the rival's best response and can shift profit toward the committed firm.

problem_text: Residents of Bridgeport are deciding whether to adopt cleaner campus shuttles. A buyer receives private benefit $35 per unit, and each unit creates an external benefit of $9 for neighbors. The marginal cost is $39.
solution:
(a) No, because private benefit $35 is below cost $39.

(b) Social benefit is $44, so the unit is efficient.

(c) A subsidy of $9 per unit makes buyers internalize the external benefit.

(d) Buyers ignore benefits received by others, so they buy too little unless policy or bargaining internalizes the spillover.

problem_text: In Portland, production of home sensors creates a pollution cost of $11 per unit. Demand is P = 126 - 2Q and private marginal cost is $23. The city is considering a Pigouvian tax.
solution:
(a) With P = private MC, Q = (126 - 23)/2 = 51.50.

(b) Social MC is $34, so Q = (126 - 34)/2 = 46.

(c) The Pigouvian tax is $11 per unit.

(d) Consumers pay demand price $34 at Q = 46.

(e) The tax makes decision makers face the external cost, reducing inefficient output toward the social optimum.

problem_text: In Providence, production of bike rentals creates a pollution cost of $13 per unit. Demand is P = 128 - 2Q and private marginal cost is $25. The city is considering a Pigouvian tax.
solution:
(a) With P = private MC, Q = (128 - 25)/2 = 51.50.

(b) Social MC is $38, so Q = (128 - 38)/2 = 45.

(c) The Pigouvian tax is $13 per unit.

(d) Consumers pay demand price $38 at Q = 45.

(e) The tax makes decision makers face the external cost, reducing inefficient output toward the social optimum.

problem_text: Two neighborhoods benefit from a public safety app. Neighborhood A's marginal benefit is MB_A = 71 - q, and neighborhood B's marginal benefit is MB_B = 60 - q. The marginal cost of improving the app is $61 per unit of quality q. The app is non-rival within the city.
solution:
(a) For a public good, sum marginal benefits vertically and set MB_A + MB_B = MC.

(b) (71 - q) + (60 - q) = 61, so q = 35.

(c) A alone sets 71 - q = 61, so q = 10.

(d) Voluntary provision is below the efficient level: 10 versus 35.

(e) Each neighborhood would like the other to pay because both benefit from the same quality improvement.

(f) Tax financing, Lindahl-style pricing, contracts, or a citywide vote can help coordinate payment.

solution_subparts.text: Voluntary provision is below the efficient level: 10 versus 35.

problem_text: A consumer has income $125 to spend on x and y. Prices are Px = $5 and Py = $7. Utility is u(x,y)=sqrt(xy), so the consumer spends half of income on each good.
solution:
(a) 5x + 7y = 125.

(b) The consumer spends $62.50 on each good.

(c) x = 12.50 and y = 8.93.

(d) Cobb-Douglas preferences value balance: as one good becomes scarce, its marginal utility rises.

solution_subparts.label: b
solution_subparts.text: The consumer spends $62.50 on each good.

solution_subparts.label: d
solution_subparts.text: Cobb-Douglas preferences value balance: as one good becomes scarce, its marginal utility rises.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Find optimal spending on each good.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Find the optimal quantities x and y.

subparts.label: d
subparts.text: Explain why the consumer does not simply buy the cheaper good only.

problem_text: A consumer with utility u(x,y)=sqrt(xy) has income $155. Initially Px = $6 and Py = $7. After a supply disruption, Px rises to $8 while income and Py stay fixed. Use the Cobb-Douglas structure to separate spending shares from quantities.
problem_title: A Price Increase with Cobb-Douglas Utility
solution:
(a) Initial x = 12.92 and y = 11.07.

(b) New x = 9.69 and y = 11.07.

(c) Spending remains $77.50 on each good.

(d) Quantity x falls from 12.92 to 9.69 because the same x spending buys fewer units.

(e) Cobb-Douglas demands have constant expenditure shares; other preferences need not keep spending shares fixed.

problem_text: A benefits office wants to provide enough vouchers for a household to reach utility u(x,y)=sqrt(xy) = 29 at minimum cost. The prices are Px = $5 and Py = $10. The office asks whether cost minimization gives the same bundle as simply buying equal quantities of both goods.
problem_title: Meeting a Utility Target at Minimum Cost
solution:
(a) Minimize 5x + 10y subject to sqrt(xy) = 29.

(b) MRS = y/x = Px/Py = 0.50, so y = 0.50x.

(c) x = u sqrt(Py/Px) = 41.01 and y = u sqrt(Px/Py) = 20.51.

(d) Minimum expenditure is $410.12.

(e) The office substitutes away from y and toward x, while still meeting the utility target.

(f) It tells us the least income needed to reach a target utility and helps decompose price-change welfare effects.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: Minimize 5x + 10y subject to sqrt(xy) = 29.

solution_subparts.label: b
solution_subparts.text: MRS = y/x = Px/Py = 0.50, so y = 0.50x.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: x = u sqrt(Py/Px) = 41.01 and y = u sqrt(Px/Py) = 20.51.

solution_subparts.label: d
solution_subparts.text: Minimum expenditure is $410.12.

solution_subparts.label: e
solution_subparts.text: The office substitutes away from y and toward x, while still meeting the utility target.

solution_subparts.label: f
solution_subparts.text: It tells us the least income needed to reach a target utility and helps decompose price-change welfare effects.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: Write the expenditure minimization problem.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Use the tangency condition to relate y and x.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Find the cost-minimizing quantities.

subparts.label: d
subparts.text: Compute minimum expenditure.

subparts.label: e
subparts.text: If Py rises, which way should the bundle adjust?

problem_text: A benefits office wants to provide enough vouchers for a household to reach utility u(x,y)=sqrt(xy) = 24 at minimum cost. The prices are Px = $6 and Py = $11. The office asks whether cost minimization gives the same bundle as simply buying equal quantities of both goods.
problem_title: Meeting a Utility Target at Minimum Cost
solution:
(a) Minimize 6x + 11y subject to sqrt(xy) = 24.

(b) MRS = y/x = Px/Py = 0.55, so y = 0.55x.

(c) x = u sqrt(Py/Px) = 32.50 and y = u sqrt(Px/Py) = 17.73.

(d) Minimum expenditure is $389.95.

(e) The office substitutes away from y and toward x, while still meeting the utility target.

(f) It tells us the least income needed to reach a target utility and helps decompose price-change welfare effects.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Civic Thread is piloting bike rentals in Providence. Weekly demand is Qd = 542 - 3P and supply is Qs = 25 + 2P, where P is the price per unit. After a campus partnership is announced, market research predicts demand will be 19 units higher at every price. Treat the market as competitive and use this setting to separate the arithmetic of equilibrium from the economic intuition.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Bike Rentals Demand Shift
solution:
(a) Set 542 - 3P = 25 + 2P. The initial price is $103.40 and quantity is 231.80.

(b) New demand is Qd = 561 - 3P, so P = $107.20 and Q = 239.40.

(c) Both sides trade more: quantity rises from 231.80 to 239.40.

(d) Demand rose at every price, creating excess demand at the old price. Competition bids price up and expands quantity.

subparts.label: b

subparts.text: State whether consumers, sellers, or both are doing more trading after the announcement.

subparts.label: d

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Juniper Labs experiments with the price of its campus shuttles. When price rises from $24 to $28, weekly sales fall from 1,126 to 874 units. Management wants more than a calculation: it wants to know whether this kind of price increase is a sensible revenue strategy.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Campus Shuttles Price Experiment
solution:
(a) Elasticity is (874 - 1126)/1126 divided by (28 - 24)/24, which equals -1.34.

(b) The absolute value is 1.34, so demand is elastic.

(c) Revenue changes from $27,024 to $24,472.

(d) Revenue decreases, so the price increase does not succeed on a revenue-only metric.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Sterling Health produces home sensors with daily cost C(q) = 140 + 15q + q^2. A team member wants to price by dividing total cost by output, while another argues that the next unit should be evaluated using marginal cost. Use q = 13 to clarify the difference.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Average Cost Versus Marginal Cost
solution:
(a) C(13) = $504.

(b) ATC = $504/13 = $38.77. AVC = (15q + q^2)/q = $28.

(c) MC(q) = 15 + 2q, so MC(13) = $41.

(d) The one-more-unit decision uses marginal cost, because fixed and already-incurred costs do not change with that unit.

solution_subparts.label: d
solution_subparts.text: The one-more-unit decision uses marginal cost, because fixed and already-incurred costs do not change with that unit.

subparts.text: Compute average total cost and average variable cost at q.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Compute marginal cost at q.

subparts.label: d
subparts.text: Explain which cost concept is relevant for deciding whether to produce one more unit.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Cobalt Cloud is the only provider of a specialized standing desks service in its region. Inverse demand is P = 324 - 2Q, marginal cost is constant at $19, and fixed cost is $100. The firm wants to understand why a monopolist does not simply serve every customer with willingness to pay above marginal cost.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Monopoly Output and Markup
solution:
(a) Revenue is 324Q - 2Q^2, so MR = 324 - 4Q.

(b) Set MR = MC: Q = 76.25 and P = $171.50.

(c) Profit is ($171.50 - $19) x 76.25 - $100 = $11,528.12.

(d) Selling more requires lowering price on marginal and inframarginal units, so the monopolist restricts quantity to keep price above MC.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

A graduating student compares two jobs. Job Safe pays $97 thousand for sure. Job Bonus pays $71 thousand plus a $43 thousand bonus with probability 60.0%. Payoffs are measured in thousands of dollars, and the student has utility u(x) = sqrt(x).
problem_title: Mixed Review: Choosing Between Job Offers
solution:
(a) EV(Bonus) = 71 + 0.6 x 43 = $96.80 thousand.

(b) A risk-neutral student chooses Job Safe because it has the higher expected value.

(c) EU(Bonus) = 9.78 and U(Safe) = 9.85.

(d) Risk-averse utility is concave, so downside risk lowers expected utility relative to a sure payment with the same expected value.

subparts.label: d
subparts.text: Explain why risk aversion can change the ranking even when expected value is higher.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Meridian Micro employs sales associates who can either help customers or hide in the stockroom. Helping creates a sale with probability 75.0%; hiding creates a sale with probability 25.0%. Effort costs the worker $5 per hour, and the outside wage is $13 per hour. Workers are risk-neutral.
solution:
(a) Helping payoff is w + S b - c.

(b) Hiding payoff is w + n b.

(c) Need (S - n)b >= c, so b = 5/(0.75 - 0.25) = $10.

(d) With a flat wage, the worker bears the effort cost but does not receive a higher payoff when effort raises the chance of sale.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

LoomLabs sells a single-use pass to two customer groups. 125 casual customers are willing to pay $59, and 83 business customers are willing to pay $90. Marginal cost is $20. The firm cannot initially identify customers, so it must set one price.
problem_title: Mixed Review: One Price or Two?
solution:
(a) Profit is ($59 - $20) x 208 = $8,112.

(b) Profit is ($90 - $20) x 83 = $5,810.

(c) The firm chooses the low price because it gives higher profit.

(d) Different groups have different willingness to pay. Charging each group closer to its value would capture more surplus if arbitrage is prevented.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Sterling Health is piloting home sensors in Portland. Weekly demand is Qd = 558 - 3P and supply is Qs = 26 + 2P, where P is the price per unit. After a campus partnership is announced, market research predicts demand will be 19 units higher at every price. Treat the market as competitive and use this setting to separate the arithmetic of equilibrium from the economic intuition.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Home Sensors Demand Shift
solution:
(a) Set 558 - 3P = 26 + 2P. The initial price is $106.40 and quantity is 238.80.

(b) New demand is Qd = 577 - 3P, so P = $110.20 and Q = 246.40.

(c) Both sides trade more: quantity rises from 238.80 to 246.40.

(d) Demand rose at every price, creating excess demand at the old price. Competition bids price up and expands quantity.

Cobalt Cloud experiments with the price of its standing desks. When price rises from $18 to $22, weekly sales fall from 1,150 to 890 units. Management wants more than a calculation: it wants to know whether this kind of price increase is a sensible revenue strategy.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Standing Desks Price Experiment
solution:
(a) Elasticity is (890 - 1150)/1150 divided by (22 - 18)/18, which equals -1.02.

(b) The absolute value is 1.02, so demand is elastic.

(c) Revenue changes from $20,700 to $19,580.

(d) Revenue decreases, so the price increase does not succeed on a revenue-only metric.

problem_text:

LoomLabs produces software seats with daily cost C(q) = 124 + 13q + q^2. A team member wants to price by dividing total cost by output, while another argues that the next unit should be evaluated using marginal cost. Use q = 9 to clarify the difference.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Average Cost Versus Marginal Cost
solution:
(a) C(9) = $322.

(b) ATC = $322/9 = $35.78. AVC = (13q + q^2)/q = $22.

(c) MC(q) = 13 + 2q, so MC(9) = $31.

(d) The one-more-unit decision uses marginal cost, because fixed and already-incurred costs do not change with that unit.

Nova Basket is the only provider of a specialized meal kits service in its region. Inverse demand is P = 332 - 2Q, marginal cost is constant at $20, and fixed cost is $90. The firm wants to understand why a monopolist does not simply serve every customer with willingness to pay above marginal cost.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Monopoly Output and Markup
solution:
(a) Revenue is 332Q - 2Q^2, so MR = 332 - 4Q.

(b) Set MR = MC: Q = 78 and P = $176.

(c) Profit is ($176 - $20) x 78 - $90 = $12,078.

(d) Selling more requires lowering price on marginal and inframarginal units, so the monopolist restricts quantity to keep price above MC.

A graduating student compares two jobs. Job Safe pays $97 thousand for sure. Job Bonus pays $73 thousand plus a $42 thousand bonus with probability 60.0%. Payoffs are measured in thousands of dollars, and the student has utility u(x) = sqrt(x).
problem_title: Mixed Review: Choosing Between Job Offers
solution:
(a) EV(Bonus) = 73 + 0.6 x 42 = $98.20 thousand.

(b) A risk-neutral student chooses Job Bonus because it has the higher expected value.

(c) EU(Bonus) = 9.85 and U(Safe) = 9.85.

(d) Risk-averse utility is concave, so downside risk lowers expected utility relative to a sure payment with the same expected value.

Vertex Coffee employs sales associates who can either help customers or hide in the stockroom. Helping creates a sale with probability 75.0%; hiding creates a sale with probability 25.0%. Effort costs the worker $5 per hour, and the outside wage is $16 per hour. Workers are risk-neutral.
solution:
(a) Helping payoff is w + S b - c.

(b) Hiding payoff is w + n b.

(c) Need (S - n)b >= c, so b = 5/(0.75 - 0.25) = $10.

(d) With a flat wage, the worker bears the effort cost but does not receive a higher payoff when effort raises the chance of sale.

Summit Seltzer can sell a basic and premium version of a service. There are 100 flexible users and 100 high-value users. Flexible users value basic at $45 and premium at $58. High-value users value basic at $57 and premium at $94. Costs are $16 for basic and $25 for premium. The firm cannot observe type.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Designing a Pricing Menu
solution:
(a) The basic price can be $45.

(b) High-value users need 94 - Ppremium >= 57 - 45, so Ppremium = $82.

(c) Menu profit is 100($45 - $16) + 100($82 - $25) = $8,600.

(d) Premium-only profit is 100($94 - $25) = $6,900.

(e) If the premium price extracts all high-type surplus, high types may prefer the basic version. The firm leaves rents to preserve self-selection.

problem_text:

Albany is considering a seller tax on software seats. Before the tax, demand is Qd = 372 - 3P and supply is Qs = 19 + 2P. The proposed policy charges sellers $7 for each unit sold. Local businesses argue they will pay the entire tax, while student groups argue that buyers will face higher prices. Use the model to evaluate both claims.
solution:
(a) Solving 372 - 3P = 19 + 2P gives P = $70.60 and Q = 160.20.

(b) With Ps = Pc - 7, set 372 - 3Pc = 19 + 2(Pc - 7). Pc = $73.40, Ps = $66.40, and Q = 151.80.

(c) Revenue is tax times quantity: $7 x 151.80 = $1,062.60.

(d) Buyers pay $2.80 more per unit and sellers receive $4.20 less per unit.

(e) The seller writes the check, but market prices adjust. Incidence depends on supply and demand responses, not on who remits the tax.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Nova Basket estimates demand for a monthly plan as Q = 676 - 4P. The current price is $30 and marginal cost is $16. A manager notices that students in class used both elasticity and markup rules to think about market power, and asks whether the current price is internally consistent with that logic.
solution:
(a) Q = 676 - 4(30) = 556.

(b) Point elasticity is (dQ/dP)(P/Q) = -4 x 30/556 = -0.22.

(c) The Lerner markup is (30 - 16)/30 = 0.47.

(d) The inverse-elasticity target is 1/0.22 = 4.63. The current markup is below that benchmark.

(e) In the inelastic region, a price increase raises revenue while reducing units and costs, so the firm would usually want to raise price.

problem_text:

Summit Seltzer is a small producer in a competitive market for portable batteries. Its short-run cost is C(q) = 160 + 21q + q^2, and the market price is $49. The fixed cost is unavoidable this month, but not in the long run. Management asks whether the firm should produce now and whether the industry should expect entry or exit later.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Production Decision with Fixed Cost
solution:
(a) MC = 21 + 2q. Setting 49 = 21 + 2q gives q = 14.

(b) Profit is Pq - C(q) = $36.

(c) The firm produces because price exceeds minimum AVC, which is $21 for this cost function.

(d) ATC = 160/q + 21 + q, minimized at q = sqrt(160); min ATC = $46.30.

(e) Since price is above min ATC, long-run forces point toward entry and falling price.

A small business has wealth of $250 thousand. A flood would cause a loss of $101 thousand with probability 2.5%. The owner has utility u(x) = ln(x), with x in thousands. Full insurance is available for a premium of $3.53 thousand.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Flood Insurance and Risk Premium
solution:
(a) EV uninsured is $247.47 thousand.

(b) CE solves ln(CE) = 2.5% ln(149) + 97.5% ln(250), so CE = $246.79 thousand.

(c) Maximum premium for full insurance is $3.21 thousand.

(d) The owner does not buy because the offered premium is above willingness to pay.

(e) Insurance removes risk. A risk-averse person may accept a lower expected monetary value in exchange for a less variable outcome.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Juniper Labs is designing a bonus contract. If an employee works hard, the success probability is 75.0%; if the employee shirks, it is 25.0%. Effort costs $5, the outside option is $14, and workers are risk-neutral. Each success creates margin $14 for the firm before labor compensation.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Base Pay and Bonus Design
solution:
(a) b* = c/(S - n) = 5/(0.75 - 0.25) = $10.

(b) w* = m - S b* + c = $11.50.

(c) Expected worker payoff is w + S b - c = $14, exactly the outside option.

(d) Firm profit with incentives is -$8.50 per worker-period; flat-wage profit is -$10.50. Incentives are worthwhile here.

(e) Noisier measurement weakens the link between effort and reward; risk aversion makes bonus pay costly because workers require compensation for income risk.

solution_subparts.label: c

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

After a period of free trade in specialty noodles, the government imposes an import tariff of $6 per unit. Domestic demand is Qd = 313 - 2P, domestic supply is Qs = 17 + 1P, and the world price is $90.67. Assume the country is small, so the world price itself does not change.
solution:
(a) Free-trade imports are 24 units.

(b) The domestic price is $96.67. Qd = 119.67, Qs = 113.67, so imports are 6.

(c) Tariff revenue is $6 x 6 = $36.

(d) Consumers lose from the higher price; domestic producers gain because they sell more at that higher price.

(e) Some lost consumer surplus becomes producer surplus or government revenue, but some becomes deadweight loss from inefficiently low consumption and high domestic production.

problem_text:

Harbor Analytics can sell a basic and premium version of a service. There are 100 flexible users and 100 high-value users. Flexible users value basic at $48 and premium at $60. High-value users value basic at $60 and premium at $88. Costs are $16 for basic and $28 for premium. The firm cannot observe type.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Designing a Pricing Menu
solution:
(a) The basic price can be $48.

(b) High-value users need 88 - Ppremium >= 60 - 48, so Ppremium = $76.

(c) Menu profit is 100($48 - $16) + 100($76 - $28) = $8,000.

(d) Premium-only profit is 100($88 - $28) = $6,000.

(e) If the premium price extracts all high-type surplus, high types may prefer the basic version. The firm leaves rents to preserve self-selection.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Stamford is considering a seller tax on portable batteries. Before the tax, demand is Qd = 380 - 3P and supply is Qs = 18 + 2P. The proposed policy charges sellers $9 for each unit sold. Local businesses argue they will pay the entire tax, while student groups argue that buyers will face higher prices. Use the model to evaluate both claims.
solution:
(a) Solving 380 - 3P = 18 + 2P gives P = $72.40 and Q = 162.80.

(b) With Ps = Pc - 9, set 380 - 3Pc = 18 + 2(Pc - 9). Pc = $76, Ps = $67, and Q = 152.

(c) Revenue is tax times quantity: $9 x 152 = $1,368.

(d) Buyers pay $3.60 more per unit and sellers receive $5.40 less per unit.

(e) The seller writes the check, but market prices adjust. Incidence depends on supply and demand responses, not on who remits the tax.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Zenith Zips estimates demand for a monthly plan as Q = 692 - 4P. The current price is $30 and marginal cost is $18. A manager notices that students in class used both elasticity and markup rules to think about market power, and asks whether the current price is internally consistent with that logic.
solution:
(a) Q = 692 - 4(30) = 572.

(b) Point elasticity is (dQ/dP)(P/Q) = -4 x 30/572 = -0.21.

(c) The Lerner markup is (30 - 18)/30 = 0.40.

(d) The inverse-elasticity target is 1/0.21 = 4.77. The current markup is below that benchmark.

(e) In the inelastic region, a price increase raises revenue while reducing units and costs, so the firm would usually want to raise price.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Harbor Analytics is a small producer in a competitive market for coffee subscriptions. Its short-run cost is C(q) = 152 + 21q + q^2, and the market price is $49. The fixed cost is unavoidable this month, but not in the long run. Management asks whether the firm should produce now and whether the industry should expect entry or exit later.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Production Decision with Fixed Cost
solution:
(a) MC = 21 + 2q. Setting 49 = 21 + 2q gives q = 14.

(b) Profit is Pq - C(q) = $44.

(c) The firm produces because price exceeds minimum AVC, which is $21 for this cost function.

(d) ATC = 152/q + 21 + q, minimized at q = sqrt(152); min ATC = $45.66.

(e) Since price is above min ATC, long-run forces point toward entry and falling price.

problem_title: Mixed Review: Market Power and Deadweight Loss
solution:
(a) Monopoly Q = 81.50 and P = $185.

(b) Competitive price equals MC = $22 and Q = 163.

(c) Variable profit is $13,284.50; after fixed cost, profit is $13,189.50.

(d) DWL is 0.5 x ($185 - $22) x (163 - 81.50) = $6,642.25.

(e) Consumers with values between MC and the monopoly price are excluded; those lost trades would have created surplus.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

A small business has wealth of $250 thousand. A flood would cause a loss of $94 thousand with probability 2.5%. The owner has utility u(x) = ln(x), with x in thousands. Full insurance is available for a premium of $3.35 thousand.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Flood Insurance and Risk Premium
solution:
(a) EV uninsured is $247.65 thousand.

(b) CE solves ln(CE) = 2.5% ln(156) + 97.5% ln(250), so CE = $247.07 thousand.

(c) Maximum premium for full insurance is $2.93 thousand.

(d) The owner does not buy because the offered premium is above willingness to pay.

(e) Insurance removes risk. A risk-averse person may accept a lower expected monetary value in exchange for a less variable outcome.

Cobalt Cloud is designing a bonus contract. If an employee works hard, the success probability is 75.0%; if the employee shirks, it is 25.0%. Effort costs $5, the outside option is $12, and workers are risk-neutral. Each success creates margin $12 for the firm before labor compensation.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Base Pay and Bonus Design
solution:
(a) b* = c/(S - n) = 5/(0.75 - 0.25) = $10.

(b) w* = m - S b* + c = $9.50.

(c) Expected worker payoff is w + S b - c = $12, exactly the outside option.

(d) Firm profit with incentives is -$8 per worker-period; flat-wage profit is -$9. Incentives are worthwhile here.

(e) Noisier measurement weakens the link between effort and reward; risk aversion makes bonus pay costly because workers require compensation for income risk.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

A small business has wealth of $250 thousand. A flood would cause a loss of $102 thousand with probability 2.5%. The owner has utility u(x) = ln(x), with x in thousands. Full insurance is available for a premium of $3.55 thousand.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Flood Insurance and Risk Premium
solution:
(a) EV uninsured is $247.45 thousand.

(b) CE solves ln(CE) = 2.5% ln(148) + 97.5% ln(250), so CE = $246.74 thousand.

(c) Maximum premium for full insurance is $3.26 thousand.

(d) The owner does not buy because the offered premium is above willingness to pay.

(e) Insurance removes risk. A risk-averse person may accept a lower expected monetary value in exchange for a less variable outcome.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Nova Basket is designing a bonus contract. If an employee works hard, the success probability is 75.0%; if the employee shirks, it is 25.0%. Effort costs $5, the outside option is $15, and workers are risk-neutral. Each success creates margin $15 for the firm before labor compensation.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Base Pay and Bonus Design
solution:
(a) b* = c/(S - n) = 5/(0.75 - 0.25) = $10.

(b) w* = m - S b* + c = $12.50.

(c) Expected worker payoff is w + S b - c = $15, exactly the outside option.

(d) Firm profit with incentives is -$8.75 per worker-period; flat-wage profit is -$11.25. Incentives are worthwhile here.

(e) Noisier measurement weakens the link between effort and reward; risk aversion makes bonus pay costly because workers require compensation for income risk.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

After a period of free trade in campus shuttles, the government imposes an import tariff of $6 per unit. Domestic demand is Qd = 329 - 2P, domestic supply is Qs = 17 + 1P, and the world price is $95. Assume the country is small, so the world price itself does not change.
solution:
(a) Free-trade imports are 27 units.

(b) The domestic price is $101. Qd = 127, Qs = 118, so imports are 9.

(c) Tariff revenue is $6 x 9 = $54.

(d) Consumers lose from the higher price; domestic producers gain because they sell more at that higher price.

(e) Some lost consumer surplus becomes producer surplus or government revenue, but some becomes deadweight loss from inefficiently low consumption and high domestic production.
