problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Nebula Noodles can sell a basic and premium version of a service. There are 100 flexible users and 100 high-value users. Flexible users value basic at $44 and premium at $58. High-value users value basic at $56 and premium at $90. Costs are $16 for basic and $24 for premium. The firm cannot observe type.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Designing a Pricing Menu
solution:
(a) The basic price can be $44.

(b) High-value users need 90 - Ppremium >= 56 - 44, so Ppremium = $78.

(c) Menu profit is 100($44 - $16) + 100($78 - $24) = $8,200.

(d) Premium-only profit is 100($90 - $24) = $6,600.

(e) If the premium price extracts all high-type surplus, high types may prefer the basic version. The firm leaves rents to preserve self-selection.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

A city council is studying the market for bike rentals after two simultaneous changes. Demand in Providence rises by 23 units at every price because commuting patterns change. At the same time, a supplier shortage lowers supply by 13 units at every price. Initially Qd = 421 - 4P and Qs = 18 + 2P. After the shock, the council considers a buyer subsidy of $6 per unit to keep the market active.
solution:
(a) Initial equilibrium is P = $67.17 and Q = 152.33.

(b) Post-shock demand is Qd = 444 - 4P and supply is Qs = 5 + 2P, so P = $73.17 and Q = 151.33.

(c) The subsidy implies Ps = Pb + 6. Solving gives Pb = $71.17, Ps = $77.17, and Q = 159.33.

(d) Government spending is $6 x 159.33 = $956.

(e) Buyers pay less out of pocket and sellers receive more per unit; the benefit is shared through price adjustments.

(f) The subsidy creates a wedge that makes purchases look cheaper to buyers and sales more attractive to sellers, so additional trades occur.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Echo Forge is evaluating two pieces of evidence before changing the price of campus shuttles. In its own price test, price fell from $32 to $29 and quantity rose from 1,348 to 1,156. In the same month, a rival raised price from $20 to $23, and Echo Forge's sales in a comparable market rose from 1,084 to 1,374. The CEO wants a recommendation that separates own-price sensitivity from substitution against rivals.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Elasticity Evidence for Pricing
solution:
(a) Arc elasticity is the percent quantity change over the percent price change using midpoints: 1.56.

(b) Revenue changes from $43,136 to $33,524.

(c) Cross-price elasticity is (1374 - 1084)/1084 divided by (23 - 20)/20 = 1.78.

(d) The cross-price elasticity is positive, so the products are substitutes.

(e) Own-price elasticity measures movement along the firm's demand curve; cross-price elasticity measures how rival prices shift demand.

(f) If revenue rose after the price cut, lowering price may be attractive; the positive cross elasticity also means rival pricing should be monitored closely.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Nebula Noodles can produce home sensors using labor L and machine time K according to q = sqrt(LK). Labor costs $19 per unit and machine time costs $28 per unit. The operations team needs q = 23 units for a contract and asks whether simply using equal amounts of L and K is cost-minimizing.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Choosing Inputs for a Contract
solution:
(a) Minimize 19L + 28K subject to sqrt(LK) = 23, or LK = 529.

(b) The tangency condition gives K/L = w/r = 0.68.

(c) L = q sqrt(r/w) = 27.92 and K = q sqrt(w/r) = 18.95.

(d) Minimized cost is $530.50 + $530.50 = $1,061.00.

(e) The firm should substitute away from labor and toward machine time. Since K/L = w/r, a higher labor wage raises the machine-time-to-labor ratio.

(f) Cost minimization equalizes marginal product per dollar, not raw units. Input prices matter.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

A public debate over Prism Produce's standing desks service considers two policies. Demand is P = 364 - 2Q, marginal cost is $24, and fixed cost is $100. Policy 1 is a per-unit tax of $10. Policy 2 is a price cap of $38. Assume the firm serves all demand at the cap if doing so covers marginal cost.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Regulating a Local Monopoly
solution:
(a) Unregulated Q = 85 and P = $194.

(b) The tax raises effective MC to $34. Q = 82.50, P = $199, and after-tax profit is $13,512.50.

(c) At the cap, Qd = (364 - 38)/2 = 163.

(d) The cap quantity is higher than monopoly quantity (163 versus 85).

(e) The price cap is more likely to expand output because it lowers price toward marginal cost, while the tax raises effective marginal cost.

(f) A well-chosen cap can reduce deadweight loss, but a cap below cost could reduce service quality, create shortages, or discourage investment.

problem_text:

A firm offers analysts a risky compensation package: base salary $91 thousand and a bonus of $58 thousand with probability 75.0%. A competing firm offers a guaranteed $115 thousand. Analysts have utility u(x)=sqrt(x). The firm is considering a smoother package with the same expected value but a smaller bonus.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Risky Pay and Smoother Contracts
solution:
(a) EV = $134.50 thousand.

(b) CE = EU^2 = $133.17 thousand.

(c) The analyst prefers the risky package because CE is above $115 thousand.

(d) Using a $20 thousand expected bonus component gives bonus $26.67 thousand and base $114.50 thousand.

(e) EU original is 11.54; EU smoother is 11.59. The smoother package is preferred by risk-averse workers.

(f) For risk-averse employees, the firm may need to pay a risk premium for volatile compensation; smoother pay can be valuable even at the same expected value.

(a) b* = $10.

(b) w* = $10.50.

(c) Effort is worth inducing when margin X >= c/(S - n) = $10.

(d) Department L margin is $9, so incentives are not worthwhile.

(e) Department H margin is $16, so incentives are worthwhile.

(f) The worker's incentive problem is the same, but the firm's benefit from extra success differs across departments.

Lawmakers are comparing a tariff and a quota in the market for standing desks. Domestic demand is Qd = 337 - 2P, domestic supply is Qs = 17 + 1P, and the world price is $99.67. A tariff of $5 would raise the domestic price to $104.67. An alternative quota would cap imports at 3 units.
solution:
(a) Free-trade imports are 21.

(b) Under the tariff, imports are 6 and revenue is $30.

(c) Set Qd - Qs = 3. The quota price is $105.67.

(d) Under the quota, Qd = 125.67 and Qs = 122.67; under the tariff, Qd = 127.67 and Qs = 121.67.

(e) Quota rents go to whoever owns the import licenses unless the government auctions them; unlike tariff revenue, they need not go to the government.

(f) The key concern is deadweight loss and rent transfer: both policies restrict mutually beneficial trade, but the quota can also transfer rents away from the public budget.

A city council is studying the market for home sensors after two simultaneous changes. Demand in Portland rises by 26 units at every price because commuting patterns change. At the same time, a supplier shortage lowers supply by 13 units at every price. Initially Qd = 429 - 4P and Qs = 19 + 2P. After the shock, the council considers a buyer subsidy of $9 per unit to keep the market active.
solution:
(a) Initial equilibrium is P = $68.33 and Q = 155.67.

(b) Post-shock demand is Qd = 455 - 4P and supply is Qs = 6 + 2P, so P = $74.83 and Q = 155.67.

(c) The subsidy implies Ps = Pb + 9. Solving gives Pb = $71.83, Ps = $80.83, and Q = 167.67.

(d) Government spending is $9 x 167.67 = $1,509.

(e) Buyers pay less out of pocket and sellers receive more per unit; the benefit is shared through price adjustments.

(f) The subsidy creates a wedge that makes purchases look cheaper to buyers and sales more attractive to sellers, so additional trades occur.

problem_text:
Mixed review prompt. Identify the relevant model before calculating; the goal is to connect the numbers to the economic logic.

Prism Produce is evaluating two pieces of evidence before changing the price of standing desks. In its own price test, price fell from $34 to $31 and quantity rose from 1,380 to 1,180. In the same month, a rival raised price from $18 to $21, and Prism Produce's sales in a comparable market rose from 1,100 to 1,398. The CEO wants a recommendation that separates own-price sensitivity from substitution against rivals.
problem_title: Mixed Review: Elasticity Evidence for Pricing
solution:
(a) Arc elasticity is the percent quantity change over the percent price change using midpoints: 1.69.

(b) Revenue changes from $46,920 to $36,580.

(c) Cross-price elasticity is (1398 - 1100)/1100 divided by (21 - 18)/18 = 1.63.

(d) The cross-price elasticity is positive, so the products are substitutes.

(e) Own-price elasticity measures movement along the firm's demand curve; cross-price elasticity measures how rival prices shift demand.

(f) If revenue rose after the price cut, lowering price may be attractive; the positive cross elasticity also means rival pricing should be monitored closely.
